hierarchyNote: Chalangin Peak is real and consistently located (Chelan County, Glacier Peak Wilderness, DaKobed Range/North Cascades, ~8,369-8,371 ft) across every source checked, so the given id/parent hierarchy is plausible. One nuance worth flagging: "Chalangin" is an informal/unofficial name (listsofjohn.com lists it in quotes, its own convention for unofficial names) for what topographic authorities generally treat as the northwest subsidiary summit of Luahna Peak, roughly 0.5 mi NW of Luahna's main summit and connected to it by a col — it only recently qualified as an independent "peak" on the Washington Top 100 prominence list after LiDAR resurveys confirmed >400 ft of prominence (409.6 ft). If the DB's hierarchy nests it as a fully independent summit unrelated to Luahna/the DaKobed group, that would be worth reconsidering, though no coordinate or identity mismatch was found.
blurb: Chalangin Peak (8,371 ft) is the northwest subsidiary summit of Luahna Peak in the DaKobed Range, a remote sub-range of the North Cascades within the Glacier Peak Wilderness, Chelan County, Washington, overlooking the headwaters of the Napeequa and White Rivers. The DaKobed peaks (Clark Mountain, Luahna, Chalangin, Tenpeak Mountain) are metamorphic-rock (schist) summits draped with small glaciers — the Pilz, Butterfly, and Richardson glaciers lie on Chalangin's north and southeast flanks. The broader Luahna massif was first climbed on August 13, 1911 by a Mazamas party (Rodney Glisan, H.H. Prouty, Winthrop Stone, and C.W. Whittlesey); Chalangin itself is an informally-named satellite summit that only recently gained recognition as a distinct "peak" after LiDAR-based resurveys confirmed sufficient topographic prominence.

routes.overview: The peak's shortest and most direct line: a long valley approach up the White River and Thunder Creek to a high camp in Chalangin Basin, followed by a snow/scramble climb up the open south slope and southwest face, through a short cliff band, to the summit.
routes.beta: From a high camp in Chalangin Basin (~5,100 ft), climbers ascend the open south slope/southwest face of the peak, passing through a rocky cliff band around 5,500-6,500 ft (one or two 4th-class moves), then traverse snow slopes above the Pilz Glacier before a cramponed finish (sources describe the final push variously as the south slope, southwest face, or southeast face depending on the exact line and year's snow coverage) to the summit. This is reported as the shortest-distance, least-elevation-gain of the three known approaches to Chalangin.
routes.approach: From the White River Trailhead (2,300 ft, end of White River Road/FR 6400), follow White River Trail #1507 to the Boulder Creek Trail junction, then continue toward Thunder Creek. Leave the trail and bushwhack up the Thunder Creek drainage (a thigh-deep creek crossing is typical in late spring), through slide alder and a 2023 burn zone (Airplane Lake Fire) with blowdowns, to a high camp/basin around 5,100 ft (Chalangin Basin) below the Luahna-Chalangin col.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent line back down the south slope/southwest face into Chalangin Basin.
routes.descentText: Downclimb the south slope/southwest face (same snow/scramble terrain, including the loose cliff band around 5,500-6,500 ft) back into Chalangin Basin, then retrace the Thunder Creek bushwhack and White River Trail to the trailhead; some parties vary the exit via Lower Thunder Meadows.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Drive to White River Trailhead (2,300 ft); hike White River Trail to the Thunder Creek drainage and bushwhack up to a high camp in Chalangin Basin (~5,100 ft). Day 2: Climb the south slope/southwest face (snow/crampon travel plus a short 4th-class cliff band) to the summit and return to camp. Day 3: Hike out via White River Trail. (Strong parties have also done the route car-to-car in a single very long day with a pre-dawn/midnight start.)
routes.bail: No fixed rappels or technical retreat are required on the standard line — the route can be abandoned at essentially any point on the open south slope/southwest face by reversing back down into Chalangin Basin.
routes.turnaround: The approach alone runs 10+ hours round trip before the summit block even begins, and the upper slopes are avalanche-prone outside of a stable summer window, so set a firm, conservative turnaround time from your start and honor it — parties who linger risk the loose cliff band or the Thunder Creek ford in the dark or in deteriorating weather.
routes.bestSeason: Late May through September is the working window. Late May-June gives a supportive snow climb via crampons (a documented ascent succeeded in this window), while a November attempt on this same line was turned back by unstable, wind-loaded snow near the top — November is also the North Cascades' wettest month. By mid-to-late summer the Thunder Creek ford drops to knee-deep or lower and the face becomes a mostly dry scramble above the cliff band.
routes.rockGrade: Class 3-4 (brief 4th-class moves through a cliff band around 5,500-6,500 ft)
objHaz: avalanche
loose rock
creek crossing
route-finding/bushwhack
knownHazards: Thunder Creek crossing runs thigh-deep and cold during spring snowmelt.
The south slope and southwest face are avalanche terrain and have turned back at least one documented attempt (November, wind-loaded fresh snow).
The cliff band around 5,500-6,500 ft has loose, low 4th-class rock.
Slide alder bushwhacking in the Thunder Creek drainage is thick and slow-going, worsened by 2023 wildfire blowdown.
watchOut: Time the Thunder Creek ford for early morning/cooler temperatures when flows are lowest.
Do not underestimate avalanche hazard on the south slope/southwest face outside a stable summer window.
The cliff band's 4th-class rock is loose — test holds carefully.
Weather can close in quickly; one trip report noted threatening storm clouds on summit day.
proTips: Aim for a stable high-pressure window in late spring/early summer when the cliff-band snow is still supportive but avalanche danger has dropped.
A whippet or second tool is useful for the steep, snow-covered final slopes.
Camping in Chalangin Basin (~5,100 ft) splits the long approach and gives a much shorter summit day than a single car-to-car push.
routes.detailedRack: No technical rock rack is needed for the standard line. Parties carry a single ice axe and crampons for the snow sections above Chalangin Basin, plus a helmet for the loose cliff band around 5,500-6,500 ft. A rope and light crevasse-rescue kit are prudent only if a variation strays onto the crevassed margin of the Pilz Glacier.
routes.proNeeds: Standard glacier/steep-snow protection (ice axe, crampons); no fixed or removable rock protection is documented as necessary on the standard south-slope/southwest-face line.
gear: Trekking poles for the creek ford
whatToBring: Gaiters
Water-crossing shoes or sandals for Thunder Creek
Bivy/camp gear if splitting the trip into 2-3 days
Bug protection for the Thunder Creek bushwhack in summer

road.name: White River Road (Forest Road 6400)
road.status: Open seasonally; mix of paved and gravel, generally passable to passenger cars in summer.
road.driveNote: From Coles Corner on US-2, drive WA-207 (Lake Wenatchee Highway) north about 10.5 miles, then continue on White River Road/FR 6400 for about 10 miles to the road-end trailhead at 2,300 ft.

climate.typical: East-slope Cascades microclimate: drier and sunnier than the west-side Cascades but still carries a substantial winter snowpack that lingers into early summer on north- and east-facing terrain.
climate.spring: Deep snowpack persists into May-June; creek fords run high and cold with snowmelt, and avalanche danger is a major hazard on the approach slopes (a November attempt on this line was turned back by unstable, wind-loaded snow).
climate.summer: The prime climbing window; the Thunder Creek ford drops to knee-deep or lower, bushwhack sections are snow-free, and the south-slope/southwest-face line becomes mostly a dry scramble above the cliff band.
climate.fall: Generally stable and dry through September; conditions deteriorate into October-November as the region's wettest, stormiest period sets in.
climate.winter: Long approach, serious avalanche terrain, and short daylight make this an atypical winter objective; not a standard-season route.

routes.corrections: Peak elevation is reported inconsistently across sources (8,320+ ft in a 1977 trip report, 8,350 ft on SummitPost, 8,366-8,369 ft on Wikipedia/onehikeaweek, 8,370.7 ft ±0.1 from a differential-GPS survey via Country Highpoints). The most precise, instrument-surveyed figure (8,370.7 ft, rounded to 8,371 ft) was used here.

routes.routeName: Little Giant Pass – Luahna Col Route
routes.overview: A longer, higher-mileage approach via Little Giant Pass and the Napeequa River valley, skirting the north side of the Pilz Glacier to reach a gully/ridge below the Luahna-Chalangin col, then a loose Class 3 scramble to the summit; often combined with an ascent of Luahna Peak (and sometimes Clark Mountain) in the same trip.
routes.beta: From a camp near Butterfly Butte in the Napeequa Valley, climbers travel through larch and pumice terrain, pass north of the crevassed Pilz Glacier, traverse granite slabs and tarns below Chalangin's north ridge, and gain the ridge via a scree gully around 8,000 ft before a final scramble over loose rock ("choss") to the summit. An alternate finish drops directly off Luahna Peak's west ridge to the Luahna-Chalangin col and climbs from there — reported as harder than expected, with some exposed but non-technical (Class 3) scrambling. A Forest Service description of this same trailhead/valley approach frames the target line as Chalangin's southeast ridge.
routes.approach: From the Little Giant Trailhead (2,600 ft) on Chiwawa River Road/FR 62, ford the Chiwawa River (no functional bridge; knee-to-thigh-deep, colder and higher earlier in the season) and climb the Little Giant Pass Trail through steep forest and meadows to the 6,409 ft pass on Chiwawa Ridge. Descend roughly 2,200 ft into the Napeequa River Valley and follow the Napeequa River (off-trail in its upper reaches) toward the Butterfly Butte area, camping around 6,200 ft.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent: scramble back down to the col/ridge, retrace the traverse below the north ridge and along the Napeequa Valley, and re-climb Little Giant Pass before dropping to the trailhead.
routes.descentText: Downclimb the loose summit scramble to the ridge/gully, traverse back below the north ridge past the Pilz Glacier's margin, drop into and cross the Napeequa Valley, then regain Little Giant Pass (6,409 ft) and descend the trail to the Chiwawa River ford and trailhead.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Little Giant Trailhead (2,600 ft) — ford Chiwawa River — over Little Giant Pass (6,409 ft) — descend into Napeequa Valley — camp near Butterfly Butte (~6,200 ft). Day 2: Traverse past the Pilz Glacier, gain the north ridge/gully, and scramble to the Chalangin summit (often combined with Luahna Peak and/or Clark Mountain in the same push); return to camp. Day 3: Hike out via Napeequa Valley and Little Giant Pass.
routes.bail: Retreat is a straightforward reversal of the ascent scramble to the ridge/col and back down the traverse into the Napeequa Valley; no fixed rappels are documented on this line.
routes.turnaround: This is a long, multi-day approach with a crevassed glacier margin and loose rock near the summit — set a turnaround time that accounts for the full traverse back to camp in daylight, and treat any deteriorating weather or wind-loaded snow near the col as a reason to retreat rather than push on.
routes.bestSeason: Late summer through fall (roughly August-October) gives the most snow-free travel; documented ascents were completed in September and October. Earlier in the season the Napeequa crossing and Pilz Glacier traverse carry more snow and higher water; a similar-approach November attempt on Chalangin's other (White River) line was turned back by avalanche conditions, underscoring that late-season storms bring the region's worst weather.
routes.rockGrade: Class 3 scrambling with exposure; loose rock ("choss") near the summit

routes.detailedRack: No fixed rock protection is documented as necessary; the summit scramble is unroped Class 3 on loose rock. Crampons and an ice axe are recommended for the Pilz Glacier traverse; a rope and basic crevasse-rescue kit are prudent given the glacier's crevassing.

road.name: Chiwawa River Road (Forest Road 62)

road.driveNote: From Coles Corner, take WA-207 north about 4.4 miles, bear onto Chiwawa Loop Road, then continue on Chiwawa River Road/FR 62 for about 19 miles to the Little Giant Trailhead (2,600 ft).

climate.typical: East-slope Cascades microclimate, drier than the west side but still holding a heavy winter snowpack with a crevassed glacier (Pilz) on the peak's north side.
climate.spring: Heavy snow cover on the pass, Napeequa Valley, and Pilz Glacier traverse; the Chiwawa River ford runs high and cold.
climate.summer: River ford eases (navigable roughly August-October per trail sources) and upper slopes lose most seasonal snow, though the glacier traverse remains.
climate.fall: Favored season for this route — documented successful trips in September and October found largely snow-free travel; larches and cooler temps but shortening daylight.
climate.winter: Not a standard-season objective; deep snow, avalanche terrain, and a long unbroken approach.

routes.corrections: Approach stats for this general approach vary between sources: a completed October trip report gives 25.6 mi round trip / ~5,770 ft gain (used here as the more precise, direct trip-report figure), while a separate November attempt account (which described the route only generally, without summiting via this line) cited roughly 27 mi / ~11,000 ft gain, likely reflecting a harder-snow year and/or a different exact line.

blurb: Booker Mountain (8,284 ft / 2,525 m) is a remote North Cascades National Park summit in Chelan County, Washington, about 1.5 miles southeast of Mount Buckner and the high point of Park Creek Ridge, between the Stehekin River and Park Creek/Thunder Creek drainages. Composed of granodioritic orthogneiss uplifted roughly 50 million years ago and later carved by Pleistocene glaciation, its long south/southwest ridge gives access to a straightforward Class 3 scramble via Horseshoe Basin or the Booker-Buckner col, while its 3,000+ foot Northeast Face — among the steepest walls in Washington — was first climbed by Dan Davis and John Holland in 1964 (reported in the 1965 American Alpine Journal). Named for Booker T. Washington by landscape painter Abby Williams Hill in 1903 and formally adopted by the USGS in 1904, it remains one of the least-visited summits near Cascade Pass, reportedly climbed only about once a year.

routes.overview: The standard route: hike the Cascade Pass/Sahale Arm trail, drop cross-country into Horseshoe Basin, traverse east below Mount Buckner, then climb the south-trending ridge/gully west of the summit to the top — a long Class 3 scramble with one short, exposed 3rd-class step.
routes.beta: Most parties combine Booker with Buckner Mountain in the same trip since both share the Cascade Pass/Sahale Arm approach and the Horseshoe Basin traverse. From Sahale Camp/Sahale Arm, descend Sahale's south side to a snow-free gully near 6,600 ft into Horseshoe Basin, traverse (staying roughly 6,400-6,600 ft) past the Buckner buttress, then follow a north-facing rock gully and permanent snowfield (7,000-7,200 ft) to the south-trending ridge west of Booker's summit. Finish up talus, slabs, and a short, somewhat exposed 3rd-class (locally 4th-class-step) gully/dike to the summit. Routefinding through Horseshoe Basin is the crux of the day, not the climbing itself.
routes.approach: Drive the Cascade River Road (paved then gravel) 23.1 miles from Marblemount to the Cascade Pass Trailhead (~3,600 ft). Hike the Cascade Pass Trail 3.7 miles/1,700 ft gain to Cascade Pass (5,392 ft), then continue up the Sahale Arm Trail toward Sahale Glacier/Sahale Camp. Leave the maintained trail near Sahale Camp and descend cross-country into Horseshoe Basin as described above.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent route back through Horseshoe Basin to Sahale Arm and the maintained trail.
routes.descentText: From the summit, retrace the south ridge/gully back down to the 7,000-7,200 ft snowfield, cross back over to the north-facing gully, and re-traverse Horseshoe Basin at 6,400-6,600 ft past the Buckner buttress to the snow-free gully below Sahale's south side (~6,600 ft). Climb back up to Sahale Arm/Sahale Camp and follow the maintained trail down through Cascade Pass to the trailhead. The Horseshoe Basin traverse is loose, slick in places, and easy to lose the correct line in both directions.
routes.itinerary: Most commonly done as one very long day from the Cascade Pass Trailhead (reported parties starting ~2-3am and finishing ~7:30pm when combining with Buckner), or as a 2-day trip with a permitted overnight at Pelton Basin or Sahale Glacier Camp to shorten the summit day.
routes.bail: The route has no fixed rappel stations; retreat is by reversing the ascent line. From the summit ridge/gully, downclimb to the 7,000-7,200 ft snowfield and back into Horseshoe Basin; from anywhere in the basin, head back toward the Sahale-side snow-free gully (~6,600 ft) and up to Sahale Arm and the maintained trail.
routes.turnaround: Set a firm turnaround time based on your pace through Horseshoe Basin — the cross-country traverse and the exposed 3rd-class summit gully are far more hazardous to reverse in the dark, in afternoon whiteout, or after summer thunderstorms soften the snowfields, and this is a long day even before adding the technical section.
routes.bestSeason: Typically climbed July through September, after the Cascade River Road opens (usually late June/early July) and once Horseshoe Basin's snowfields have consolidated enough to cross safely; early-season attempts face more snow travel and less obvious routefinding, while late-season attempts may find some slopes melted to loose scree/slab.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage in the North Cascades National Park backcountry around Cascade Pass, Sahale Arm, or Horseshoe Basin; carry a satellite messenger for emergencies.

road.name: Cascade River Road

climate.typical: Interior North Cascades alpine climate: substantial winter snowpack, a short July-September climbing window, and the potential for afternoon thunderstorms in summer.
climate.spring: Trailhead access blocked by the gated, snow-covered Cascade River Road into June; the route above Cascade Pass is heavily snow-covered.
climate.summer: Primary climbing season; snowfields in Horseshoe Basin and near the summit gully consolidate through July and August.
climate.fall: Good conditions into September before the road/trail become snow-affected again; shorter days.
climate.winter: Road gated at Eldorado (MP 20); the peak is effectively inaccessible without a major ski/snowshoe approach.

access.permit: A backcountry permit is required for any overnight stay in the park; permits are limited and reservable via Recreation.gov, with walk-up availability at the Wilderness Information Center in Marblemount

routes.corrections: Summit elevation is given as 8,284 ft by Wikipedia/USGS-derived sources, 8,280 ft by some peakbagging sites, and 8,286 ft by one trip-report source; this entry uses the USGS-sourced 8,284 ft as canonical. The onehikeaweek.com source's own stated trailhead-to-summit elevation range (3,480'-8,286') was used to compute gainFt/distKm for this specific route rather than mixing figures across sources.

routes.overview: An alternate Class 3 scramble reached from Park Creek Pass via the Booker-Buckner col, on the mountain's northwest side; used mainly by parties already in the Park Creek/Buckner area on a multi-day traverse rather than as a dedicated approach.
routes.beta: From a camp at or near Park Creek Pass, ascend toward the Booker-Buckner col, crossing a broad sandy gully around 6,900 ft roughly a mile from the pass; a few crevasses/cracks near the col are typically bypassed on snowbridges. From the col, the northwest side of Booker is mostly Class 3 scrambling with some easy Class 2-3 terrain to the summit.
routes.approach: Reached via a multi-day backpack up the Thunder Creek Trail from Colonial Creek Campground to Park Creek Pass (roughly 40 miles round trip with on the order of 7,000 ft of cumulative gain including the bonus climb to Junction Camp), or as part of a Ptarmigan-style traverse linking Cascade Pass/Buckner/Park Creek Pass. From a Park Creek Pass-area camp, it is a short day-trip scramble up to the Booker-Buckner col and on to the summit.
routes.descent: Reverse the route back over the Booker-Buckner col and down to Park Creek Pass.
routes.descentText: Downclimb the northwest-side Class 3 terrain back to the Booker-Buckner col, then descend the broad sandy gully (using snowbridges over any crevasses/cracks as on the ascent) back toward Park Creek Pass camp.
routes.itinerary: Typically a multi-day trip: Day 1 backpack in via Thunder Creek to a Park Creek Pass-area camp; Day 2 day-climb Booker (often combined with Buckner) via the Booker-Buckner col; Day 3+ hike out or continue a traverse.
routes.bail: Retreat by reversing the route to the Booker-Buckner col and down into the broad gully toward Park Creek Pass; there are no fixed anchors or rappels on this route.
routes.turnaround: Because the col crossing depends on snowbridges over a bergschrund/crevasses, turn around well before deteriorating snow conditions (warm afternoons, recent precipitation) make those crossings unsafe, and allow enough daylight to be back at the col before conditions soften further.
routes.bestSeason: Best when snowbridges near the Booker-Buckner col are still intact but consolidated, generally mid-to-late summer; conditions change year to year and can degrade significantly within days, so check recent trip reports.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage in the Thunder Creek/Park Creek Pass backcountry; carry a satellite messenger.

routes.fa: Dan Davis and John Holland, August 1964 (published in the 1965 American Alpine Journal)
routes.overview: A rarely-repeated alpine rock route up the prominent central rib of Booker's 3,000+ foot Northeast Face, one of the steepest walls in Washington, first climbed via an icefall approach and a crumbly mid-face rock band to a notch just below the summit.
routes.beta: From a camp near the head of Park Creek, climb easier cliffs to a small glacier at the base of the face, then traverse left across an icefall (reported as a genuinely exposed, 'frightening' crossing) to reach the main rock wall. Follow a prominent central rib essentially straight up the face on generally sound Cascade granodiorite/orthogneiss, through a crumbly red rock stratum that crosses the whole face at roughly mid-height, to a 100-foot notch near the summit, then on to the top. The face averages about 70 degrees and is over 3,000 feet high.
routes.approach: Approach via a multi-day backpack toward Park Creek Pass/the head of Park Creek (from the Thunder Creek Trail out of Colonial Creek Campground, or as part of a Buckner-area traverse), then camp near the base of the face for an alpine start.
routes.descentText: From the summit, descend the ordinary route down to the Booker-Buckner col and back toward a Park Creek-area camp — the same descent used by the first-ascent party, who found their camp again just before dark.
routes.itinerary: Multi-day: approach and establish camp near Park Creek/the base of the face; climb the face in a long single push; descend via the Booker-Buckner col to camp; hike out.
routes.bail: No fixed rappel anchors are documented on this route; retreat below the crux red-rock band would require downclimbing back through the icefall traverse, which is itself hazardous — parties should commit only with strong confidence in completing the climb and descending via the col.
routes.turnaround: Given the icefall traverse and long, sustained face climbing, turn around early enough to be off the technical terrain and past the icefall crossing well before dark or before afternoon warming further destabilizes the glacier/icefall.
routes.bestSeason: Climbed historically in early-to-mid August; conditions on the approach glacier/icefall and any residual snow/ice in the summit gully are season- and year-dependent, so this route likely climbs best in a similar window with a stable, well-bridged icefall.
routes.comms: No cell coverage in the Park Creek/Booker area; carry a satellite messenger.
routes.rockGrade: 5.8 (reported as NCCS old-system grade IV, F8; some sections class 3-4)
gear: Ice axe/tools for the glacier and icefall approach
Light alpine rock rack (nuts, small-to-mid cams)
whatToBring: Bivy/camping gear for the multi-day approach
Backcountry permit
Extra care/gear for the icefall crossing (may warrant a rope even on the approach)
routes.detailedRack: A light alpine rock rack (set of nuts, cams to roughly 3 inches) for the class 4-5 rib, plus glacier travel gear (axe, crampons, and consider pickets/screws) for the approach glacier and icefall traverse.
routes.proNeeds: Standard trad protection for the class 4-5 rib climbing; extra care placing protection in the crumbly red stratum band at mid-height, which the first-ascent party specifically noted as looser than the otherwise sound surrounding rock.
proTips: Rock quality was reported as typical, sound Cascade granodiorite outside the mid-face red stratum band — don't assume the whole face is loose
The icefall traverse below the face was the crux of the approach, not the climbing itself; assess it carefully before committing
This is a very rarely repeated route — expect no fixed gear, no beta trail, and to be routefinding largely from the original 1964 account
watchOut: Exposed icefall traverse required just to reach the base of the rock face

A 100-foot notch obstacle just below the summit
knownHazards: Icefall/glacier hazard on the approach (crevasses, serac fall)
Rockfall potential from the loose red-rock band
Extreme remoteness — any incident on a 3,000-foot face this rarely climbed would be very difficult to get help for

hierarchyNote: Sanity check passed with an important caveat: "Alpine Lookout" is a real, identifiable 6,237 ft (per WTA/SummitPost/Wenatchee Outdoors) / 6,235 ft (per WillhiteWeb/AllTrails/PeakVisor) knob on the eastern end of Nason Ridge in the Central Cascades (Chelan County, Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest), named for the still-staffed 1936/1975 fire lookout on its summit — coordinates and the Nason Ridge location are well corroborated across peakvisor, WTA, SummitPost, and multiple trip-report sites. HOWEVER, this is a maintained Forest Service trail hike (Round Mountain Trail #1529 -> Nason Ridge Trail #1583, non-technical, no exposure beyond one narrow ridge saddle), not a rock/ice/alpine climbing objective — it has NO Mountain Project page (MP's "Nason Ridge" entries are unrelated bolted granite crags miles away near Leavenworth) and no technical route data exists on Peakbagger/SummitPost beyond hiking trip reports. I filled the "standard route" as the hiking trail to the summit per the enrichment pattern used for other scramble/walk-up WA peaks in this catalog, but nearly all technical-climbing fields (rock/ice/alpine/aid grade, pitches, rack, pitchDetail, commitment grade) are genuinely null because none apply — this is not a climbing route. Flagging for review in case this peak doesn't belong in a technical-climbing catalog at all. Peakbagger.com and ListsOfJohn.com both blocked direct fetch (403) during research; their data was cross-checked via PeakVisor (which cites Peakbagger-style stats) and WTA/SummitPost instead.
blurb: Alpine Lookout is a 6,237-ft knob on the eastern end of Nason Ridge in the Central Cascades near Stevens Pass/Lake Wenatchee, Chelan County, Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest. It carries one of Washington's last regularly staffed fire lookouts — an L-4 cab built in 1936 and replaced by the current 15'x15' R-6 flat cab in 1975/1976, listed on the National Historic Lookout Register — and is reached entirely by maintained non-technical trail rather than any rock or ice climbing route.

routes.overview: A non-technical ridge-walk trail hike from the east side of Nason Ridge, climbing steadily through recovering burn forest to a saddle, then following the ridge crest to the historic Alpine Lookout at the summit.
routes.beta: The standard and shortest approach to Alpine Lookout starts at the Round Mountain Trailhead off FR 6910 east of Stevens Pass. Round Mountain Trail #1529 climbs steeply for about the first 1.6 miles through forest and an older burn area, then joins Nason Ridge Trail #1583. The trail undulates along the crest of Nason Ridge, crosses the exposed 'Ninemile Saddle' knife-edge around mile 4.5, and a final 0.3-mile summit spur leads to the staffed fire lookout at 6,237 ft. A longer alternative approaches from the west via Merritt Lake Trail #1588 to Nason Ridge Trail #1583 (about 11.5-12 miles one-way between trailheads, ~4,400 ft gain from that side), used mainly for through-hikes or when the Round Mountain side is inaccessible.
routes.approach: From Leavenworth, drive US Highway 2 west approximately 17.4-17.7 miles (or from Stevens Pass, drive east about 18 miles) to just past the Nason Creek Rest Area. Turn north (right heading west / left heading east) onto the unsigned Butcher Creek Road, Forest Road 6910 -- watch for the turn on a curve with fast highway traffic. Follow FR 6910 (good gravel) for about 4.5 miles, then turn onto spur FR 6910-170 and continue roughly 0.1-0.25 mile to the Round Mountain Trailhead.
routes.descent: Descend the same trail: Nason Ridge Trail #1583 back to the Round Mountain Trail #1529 junction, then down Round Mountain Trail to the trailhead.
routes.descentText: From the lookout, retrace the 0.3-mile summit spur down to Nason Ridge Trail #1583, follow the ridge trail back across Ninemile Saddle, and descend Round Mountain Trail #1529 through the burn area and forest to the Round Mountain Trailhead on FR 6910. No technical descent gear is needed; the main concerns are the exposed knife-edge saddle in poor weather/ice and the sustained steep first/last 1.6 miles.
routes.itinerary: Single long day: trailhead to lookout is commonly done as a ~9.6-10.2 mile round trip with roughly 4-7 hours of hiking depending on pace and side-trips; some parties camp along Nason Ridge (e.g. near Rock Lake or Crescent Lake, accessed via side trails) to split it into an overnight.
routes.bail: The route follows a single maintained ridge trail with no technical terrain; retreat at any point is simply hiking back down Nason Ridge Trail to the Round Mountain Trail junction and out to the trailhead. There is no fixed rappel or bail-anchor infrastructure.
routes.turnaround: Because the crux is a mid-route exposed knife-edge saddle (Ninemile Saddle) and the trail gains most of its elevation in the first ~1.6 miles, parties should set a turnaround time based on remaining daylight and current weather/visibility, and turn back before crossing exposed ridge sections in high wind, thunderstorms, or icy/snow-covered conditions rather than push through deteriorating conditions to reach the lookout.
routes.bestSeason: June through October is the typical window. Alpine Lookout is one of the first high points on Nason Ridge to shed its winter snowpack, making it a good early-summer objective; wildflowers (paintbrush, delphinium) peak in July-August and fall color is notable in September-October. It is also visited as a winter/spring snowshoe objective by experienced parties, though the trail is not maintained or avalanche-mitigated in winter.
routes.comms: No official cell coverage data found; the lookout itself is staffed with Forest Service radio/phone communication in summer, but hikers should not rely on cell service along the ridge trail.
routes.proNeeds: No technical protection is used on this route; it is a Class 1 maintained trail hike with no climbing gear required.
gear: Extra water (trail is dry with minimal water sources)
whatToBring: Northwest Forest Pass or Interagency (America the Beautiful) pass for trailhead parking
Map/GPS (trail junctions with Snowy Creek, Rock Mountain, and Merritt Lake trails)
Layers for exposed, windy ridge sections
Sufficient water -- only one seasonal spring reported near the trailhead
Ice axe/traction if traveling in snow-covered spring/early-summer or shoulder-season conditions
proTips: The first ~1.6 miles on Round Mountain Trail #1529 up to the Nason Ridge Trail junction are the steepest part of the hike; most of the elevation gain happens early.
Alpine Lookout is one of the earliest high points on Nason Ridge to lose its snow, making it a good target when higher Cascades peaks are still snowed in.
The lookout is staffed by a Forest Service volunteer/employee most summers -- visitors are generally welcome to chat but should respect it as an active duty station.
watchOut: Ninemile Saddle (~mile 4.5) is a narrow, roughly ten-foot-wide knife-edge ridge crossing steep, rock-filled gullies on both sides -- use caution in wind or when wet/icy.
Mountain goats frequent the summit area and can approach closely; keep well clear and do not feed or approach them.
Water is scarce along the entire route -- carry all you need.
South-facing slopes between Alpine Lookout and Merritt Lake see significant avalanche activity in spring and are not suitable for travel without avalanche-aware routefinding/gear in that season.
knownHazards: Exposed narrow ridge crossing at Ninemile Saddle with steep drop-offs on both sides.
Limited/no reliable water sources along the trail.
Avalanche-prone south-facing slopes on the Merritt Lake side of Nason Ridge in spring conditions.
Resident mountain goat population near the summit.

climate.winter: Snow-covered and unmaintained; accessed only by experienced snowshoers/skiers, with the access road often not plowed.

hierarchyNote: Confirmed as a real, distinct peak: Mount Howard, 7,063 ft (listsofjohn.com lists 7,070 ft — likely a LiDAR remeasurement; both figures appear in reputable sources), Chelan County WA, at approx. 47.8144°N, -120.9547°W. It is the highest point of Nason Ridge, between Stevens Pass and Lake Wenatchee, ~10 mi east of Stevens Pass, on Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest land NORTH of US-2/Stevens Pass — i.e. outside (north of) the Alpine Lakes Wilderness boundary, which runs along US-2 to the south. Do not confuse with (1) Mount Howard in the Wallowa Mountains, Oregon (the well-known tramway peak near Enterprise, OR), or (2) the Mountain Project "Nason Ridge" crag area, which is a roadside bolted sport-climbing area near Leavenworth and is a different feature from this summit despite the shared name — no technical rock/ice/alpine routes exist on Mount Howard itself; it is climbed exclusively as a Class 2-3 off-trail scramble. Parent hierarchy (Nason Ridge / Wenatchee Mountains / Chelan County) is geographically plausible and consistent with all sources checked.
blurb: Mount Howard (7,063 ft) is the highest summit on Nason Ridge in Chelan County, Washington, standing between Stevens Pass and Lake Wenatchee on Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest land just north of US Highway 2. Composed of Late Cretaceous migmatitic gneiss of the Nason Terrane, it is a non-technical but long and strenuous peak — with 2,903 ft of prominence — most commonly reached via an off-trail scramble up its broad south slope from Crescent Lake. The mountain's name was likely applied by an early survey party and first appeared in print in 1887; no first-ascent record has been found.

routes.overview: The standard and easiest line to Mount Howard's summit: a long off-trail approach via the Nason Ridge Trail to Crescent Lake, followed by a Class 2-3 scramble up the peak's broad, open south-facing slope and a short loose gully to the summit ridge.
routes.beta: Most parties reach Crescent Lake, which sits below Howard's south side along the Nason Ridge Trail, from either the Merritt Lake Trailhead (shorter, more direct) or the Rock Mountain Trailhead via Rock Lake and the Rock-Crescent col (longer, more scenic, and commonly combined with Rock Mountain). From Crescent Lake's NE side, ascend north up open talus and meadow slopes; the upper mountain narrows into a loose, dirty class 3 gully before easing back to talus and a climbers' trail to the summit. Many parties combine the ascent with a traverse to neighboring Mount Mastiff (about 1 mile away) or Rock Mountain for a full Nason Ridge outing.
routes.approach: Two common trailheads on US-2 in Chelan County give access. Merritt Lake Trailhead (3,000 ft, high-clearance/AWD recommended on the access road): from Leavenworth take US-2 west about 24 miles (or from Stevens Pass, east about 11.5 miles) to milepost 76, turn onto Forest Road 657, and continue 1.5 miles to the trailhead; hike the Merritt Lake Trail (2,000 ft gain in 2.5 miles) then join the Nason Ridge Trail east to Crescent Lake. Rock Mountain Trailhead (2,650-2,675 ft, fee area): on US-2 near milepost 73, about 27 miles west of Leavenworth / 8.5 miles east of Stevens Pass; follow the Rock Mountain Trail up to Rock Lake (snow persists above ~5,000 ft into late spring), then over the Rock-Crescent col (~6,500 ft) and down about 900 ft to Crescent Lake. From either approach, water is available at Crescent Lake but is unreliable higher on the mountain.
routes.descent: Reverse the south slope ascent line back down to Crescent Lake and out the Nason Ridge Trail to the chosen trailhead.
routes.descentText: Downclimb the loose class 3 gully on the upper south face with care — it is dirty and holds are unreliable in both directions. Below that, descend talus and open slopes back to Crescent Lake. In early-season (spring) conditions the col/gully snow can refreeze firm overnight, making crampons and an ice axe useful for a controlled descent; some parties glissade snow-filled sections when soft. From Crescent Lake, retrace the Nason Ridge Trail to the trailhead, watching for the correct drainage — parties have mistakenly dropped toward Royal Creek from the ridge.
routes.itinerary: Commonly done car-to-car in a single long day (about 7.5-8.75 hours round trip reported from Merritt Lake TH for Howard alone or Howard+Mastiff combined). A 2-day version is also used, especially for spring snow climbs or when combining multiple Nason Ridge summits: Day 1 — trailhead to a bivy near Crescent Lake or the summit (about 12 miles, 6,000-6,400 ft gain); Day 2 — summit (if not reached Day 1) and return to the trailhead.
routes.bail: Retreat is straightforward at any point on this non-technical route — reverse the ascent line to Crescent Lake and the Nason Ridge Trail. The only real crux is the short loose class 3 gully on the south face, which can be downclimbed in dry conditions; in spring, a party turned back by firm snow or cornice hazard on the Rock-Crescent col can retreat the way they came without committing to the peak.
routes.turnaround: The approach alone is long (roughly 12-14.5 miles and 5,000-6,400 ft of gain depending on trailhead, with significant intermediate elevation loss/regain over the Rock-Crescent col), and much of it is off-trail through look-alike bowls and talus above Crescent Lake. Set a turnaround time that leaves ample daylight to retrace the off-trail sections and the loose summit gully before dark — parties have needed a headlamp for the final hour even on a full-day start.
routes.bestSeason: Most commonly climbed as a dry-season scramble from July through September, when the route is mostly snow-free talus and trail. It is also a popular spring snow climb in late April through May/early June, when consolidated snow fills the approach gullies and col, allowing efficient booting/step-kicking (crampons and ice axe recommended, especially for a firm-snow descent); trip reports from this window note 12-18 inches of soft snow over a firm base and cornice/slough hazard near the Rock Mountain col.

climate.typical: East-slope Cascades climate on the Nason Ridge crest: notably drier and sunnier than the Cascade crest and west-side valleys due to its position on the leeward/eastern side of the range, with hot summer days and cold, snowy winters.
climate.spring: Variable, often deep snow persisting above ~5,000 ft into May; suitable for snow climbing with proper gear.
climate.summer: Warm and mostly dry July-September; the standard window for the dry scramble.
climate.fall: Cooling and dry through September, with increasing chance of early-season snow by October.
climate.winter: Cold with heavy snow accumulation; not a standard climbing season for this route.

blurb: Dark Peak is a remote 8,507 ft (2,593 m) summit in the Cascade Range in Chelan County, Washington, part of the "Stehekin Triangle" cluster of peaks bounded by Lake Chelan, the Stehekin River, Agnes Creek, and Railroad Creek, roughly 2.2 km northwest of its taller neighbor Bonanza Peak. It sits within the Glacier Peak Wilderness (Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest), reached only via the roadless Stehekin Valley from Lake Chelan, and carries the small, crevassed Dark Glacier on its north side. Despite modest topographic prominence (roughly 256-264 ft), it ranks 56th on Washington's "Bulger" hundred-highest-peaks list and is climbed as a glacier route with scrambling near the summit rather than technical rock climbing.

routes.overview: The standard route climbs Dark Peak's north-side Dark Glacier from a remote upper basin above Swamp Creek, then crosses a bergschrund/moat onto the summit rock ridge for a short scramble to the top -- a moderate glacier climb whose real difficulty is the long, brushy, multi-day approach rather than the technical climbing.
routes.beta: Dark Peak is regarded as an adventure/remoteness objective more than a technically hard one. After a boat-and-shuttle approach into the roadless Stehekin Valley, parties hike the Agnes Creek Trail (PCT) to a high camp, then leave the trail for a long, routefinding-heavy cross-country push -- either up the historically brushy, officially abandoned Swamp Creek climbers' path, or via a newer variant farther south on the PCT to Hemlock Camp -- gaining a basin below "Dark Fin Col." From the col, parties rope up, descend a loose slope onto the crevassed Dark Glacier, pick a line across it (the right side of the glacier is reported as a good line), and finish with a belayed crossing of the summit moat/bergschrund onto the rock ridge leading to the top. Reported early-to-mid-summer conditions give firm, well-bridged snow with minimal crevasse danger; by late summer more crevasses are exposed and the glacier becomes less stable.
routes.approach: Access begins in Chelan via the Lady of the Lake passenger ferry (Lady Express ~2.5 hrs or Lady II ~4 hrs) or floatplane to the roadless village of Stehekin, then a scheduled valley shuttle bus/van up the Stehekin Valley Road to High Bridge (~1,700 ft), the trailhead for the Agnes Creek Trail/PCT and the end of the road. From High Bridge there are two documented approach options to the base of the peak: (1) Traditional Swamp Creek approach -- hike the Agnes Creek Trail (PCT) south about 5-5.5 miles to Fivemile Camp (2,200 ft), continuing about 3 more miles (roughly 8-9 miles total from High Bridge) to Swamp Creek Camp (2,800 ft); leave the maintained trail and follow the long-unmaintained, heavily brushed Swamp Creek climbers' path upvalley, crossing Swamp Creek on a large log to its east side and pushing through roughly a quarter mile of thick slide alder and vine maple, to reach a lower basin (often not until after dark for many parties); continue cross-country from the lower basin to the upper basin (about 4-5 hours of further travel, reaching roughly 4,300 ft), then kick steps up around 3,000 ft of steep, often snow-covered terrain (no established track) to the saddle/col below the glacier. (2) Newer PCT/Hemlock Camp variant -- continue farther south on the PCT roughly 12.5 miles from High Bridge to Hemlock Camp, then leave the trail and climb northwest through open forest and slopes (about 3,500 ft of gain) directly to Dark Fin Col, avoiding most of the Swamp Creek brush; this is reported as a substantially easier approach even though it is not the route most guidebooks still describe as standard.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent: down-climb/re-cross the summit moat back onto the Dark Glacier, descend the glacier roped (favoring the same clean line used on the way up), regain Dark Fin Col and the upper basin, then retrace either the Swamp Creek bushwhack or the PCT/Hemlock Camp variant back to camp and out to High Bridge.
routes.descentText: There is no separate descent route documented -- parties down-climb the same line they climbed. From the summit, reverse the short scramble to the moat, get a belay or careful downclimb across the bergschrund/moat back onto glacier ice, and descend the Dark Glacier roped as a team, staying alert for crevasses that may be less obvious on the way down and for wet, loose rock along the glacier's rock margins. Regain Dark Fin Col, then descend the approach terrain (loose/steep slopes into the basin, and either the Swamp Creek brush or the PCT/Hemlock Camp slopes) back to high camp. Given how long and strenuous the round trip from camp is, most parties plan to be back at camp well before dark and treat the final hike out to High Bridge as a separate day to make the fixed ferry/shuttle departure.
routes.itinerary: Commonly done as a 3-day trip: Day 1 -- ferry/floatplane to Stehekin, shuttle to High Bridge, hike in to Fivemile Camp or Swamp Creek Camp (or toward Hemlock Camp on the alternate approach). Day 2 -- long, strenuous summit day: cross-country travel and bushwhacking/scrambling to Dark Fin Col, roped travel across the Dark Glacier, belayed moat crossing, short scramble to the summit, then reverse the entire route back to high camp. Day 3 -- hike out to High Bridge and catch the return shuttle/ferry to Chelan.
routes.bail: Retreat is straightforward in concept but slow in practice: reverse the route back across the moat and down the Dark Glacier to Dark Fin Col and the upper basin, then descend into the brush/forest to regain the Agnes Creek Trail (PCT). Once back on the maintained trail, the only realistic way out is to hike to High Bridge -- there is no shorter escape route given the valley's roadless, remote setting.
routes.turnaround: Given the long approach, the extensive bushwhacking or cross-country travel, and roped glacier travel with crevasse and moat hazards, parties should set and honor a firm turnaround time keyed to remaining daylight and weather rather than pushing to reach the summit -- both the brush/scree descent and the glacier/moat crossing are markedly slower and more hazardous after dark or in deteriorating weather.
routes.bestSeason: Trip reports and guide sources point to a core early-to-mid-summer window (roughly June into August) as the sweet spot: seasonal snow still eases travel in the upper basin and on the steep slopes below Dark Fin Col, and the Dark Glacier's crevasses tend to be more consistently snow-bridged, giving firmer "styrofoam" snow conditions with comparatively low crevasse danger. By late summer and fall (August-October) more crevasses are exposed and the glacier becomes less stable, while earlier in spring the approach and glacier remain fully snow-covered. WTA broadly lists Dark Peak as reasonably climbable May through October.
routes.comms: No cell phone reception exists anywhere in the Stehekin Valley or on the route to Dark Peak; a public payphone is available at Stehekin Landing. Given the remoteness and the fact that NPS's Golden West Visitor Center in Stehekin has had reduced or no seasonal ranger staffing in recent years, carrying a satellite communicator/messenger is strongly advised.
routes.detailedRack: A standard glacier-travel rack rather than a rock rack: one rope per rope team, harnesses for all climbers, 2-3 pickets and/or ice screws for crevasse-rescue anchors and to belay the short summit moat crossing, ice axes, crampons, and helmets. A basic crevasse-rescue kit (prusik cords or mechanical ascenders, a pulley, extra slings and locking carabiners) should be carried by each rope team. Little to no traditional rock protection is used on the standard line; the final scrambling is generally unroped in good conditions.
routes.proNeeds: Protection needs on this route are glacier-travel oriented rather than rock-gear oriented: pickets or ice screws for crevasse-rescue anchors and a body or picket belay for the short, exposed moat/bergschrund crossing onto the summit ridge are the main technical protection used; the summit scramble itself is typically climbed unroped.
gear: Crevasse rescue kit (prusiks/ascenders, pulley, slings, locking biners)
whatToBring: Ten Essentials
Bug repellent and long sleeves (brushy approach)
Water treatment/filter
Bear-resistant food storage per wilderness regulations
Satellite communicator (no cell coverage)
Sturdy pants/gaiters for bushwhacking
proTips: Consider the newer approach via the PCT to Hemlock Camp (~12.5 miles from High Bridge) and then northwest through open forest to Dark Fin Col -- multiple parties report it avoids most of the historically brutal Swamp Creek brush that most guidebooks still describe as standard.
Climb early-to-mid season (June-July) for firmer, better snow-bridged glacier conditions and to avoid the more exposed crevasses reported in late summer.
Scout the right side of the Dark Glacier, which several trip reports describe as the cleaner line through the crevasses.
Book the Lake Chelan ferry and Stehekin Valley shuttle well ahead of time -- the whole itinerary is built around their fixed, limited daily schedules.
watchOut: The Swamp Creek 'climbers path' is officially abandoned and extremely brushy; several parties have not reached the lower basin until after dark because they underestimated this leg.
The Dark Glacier is genuinely crevassed -- rope up and do not assume a given line is safe without current-condition routefinding.
The final moat/bergschrund crossing onto the summit ridge is typically the technical crux of the day and is usually belayed.
There is no cell coverage anywhere on the approach or climb; carry a satellite communicator.
knownHazards: Crevasses on the Dark Glacier require roped travel and active routefinding, especially later in the season.
The Swamp Creek approach involves a log crossing of Swamp Creek and roughly a quarter mile of dense slide alder/vine maple brush.
Loose, wet rock is present below snow slabs near the glacier margins and around Dark Fin Col.
The summit moat/bergschrund crossing is exposed and typically requires a belay.
Extreme remoteness (boat/floatplane-only access, no road, no cell coverage) magnifies the consequences of any injury or delay.

road.name: Stehekin Valley Road
road.status: Open from Stehekin Landing to High Bridge as of the 2026 season, following major December 2025 flood/debris-flow damage; NPS trail crews were still reconstructing damaged sections park-wide as of mid-2026
road.driveNote: No public highway connects Stehekin to the outside road network. Access is by Lake Chelan Boat Company passenger ferry (Lady Express ~2.5 hrs or Lady II ~4 hrs) or floatplane from Chelan, followed by the Stehekin Valley shuttle bus/van (~$10/adult each way) to High Bridge, the trailhead and end of the road.

climate.forecastZone: North Cascades National Park Complex / Chelan County east-slope Cascades backcountry (NWS Spokane forecast area)
climate.typical: Cool, changeable alpine-glacier weather typical of the eastern North Cascades crest; the Dark Glacier basin holds snow well into summer
climate.spring: Approach and upper basin/glacier remain fully snow-covered; travel to the peak is not practical until access opens and snow consolidates
climate.summer: Prime climbing window; June-July trip reports describe firm 'styrofoam' snow and well-bridged crevasses, becoming warmer and buggier on the brushy approach as summer progresses
climate.fall: By August-October, more crevasses are exposed on the Dark Glacier and glacier stability decreases per trip reports
climate.winter: Not a winter climbing objective; Stehekin Valley Road/shuttle and ferry access are limited in the off-season

access.landManager: Glacier Peak Wilderness, Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest (Chelan Ranger District) on the peak and upper basin/glacier; the High Bridge/Agnes Creek Trail approach corridor lies within the Lake Chelan National Recreation Area, North Cascades National Park Service Complex
access.fees: No fee for wilderness travel or camping itself; the Stehekin Valley shuttle bus fare is roughly $10/adult each way, and the Lake Chelan ferry/floatplane fare is separate and not free
access.permit: A free NPS backcountry/wilderness camping permit is required for designated campsites along the Agnes Creek corridor (Fivemile Camp, Swamp Creek Camp); permits have historically been issued in Stehekin at the Golden West Visitor Center, which has had reduced/no seasonal ranger staffing in recent years -- confirm the current permit process with North Cascades National Park before the trip
access.closures: Stehekin Valley Road and area trails sustained significant damage from December 2025 atmospheric-river flooding and debris flows; an interim elevated road section (Company Creek Road) was completed by June 2026, but NPS trail crews were still reconstructing damaged trails park-wide as of mid-2026 -- check the park's Trail Conditions page and Stehekin flood-update page before attempting this route

timing.recommendedStart: Alpine (pre-dawn) start from high camp given the length of the summit day, which includes off-trail travel, roped glacier crossing, and a belayed moat crossing before and after the summit

emergency.sheriffDispatch: 911, or RiverCom regional dispatch 509-663-9911, routed to Chelan County Sheriff's Office / Chelan County Volunteer Search & Rescue
emergency.rangerStation: Golden West Visitor Center, Stehekin, 509-699-2080 ext. 14 (seasonal and, in recent years, intermittent/reduced staffing -- do not rely on it being staffed)
emergency.nearestHospital: Lake Chelan Community Hospital, Chelan, WA -- reachable from Stehekin only by boat, floatplane, or helicopter; serious backcountry injuries in this area are typically evacuated by helicopter
emergency.notes: This is one of the most remote standard-route objectives in the WA catalog: no road access (boat/floatplane only to Stehekin), no cell phone reception anywhere in the valley or on route (public payphone at Stehekin Landing only), and a multi-day commitment before any rescue resource can reach the area. A satellite communicator is strongly recommended.

blurb: Bears Breast Mountain (7,197 ft) is a rugged, seldom-visited summit in the Alpine Lakes Wilderness of the central Washington Cascades, about 10 miles northeast of Snoqualmie Pass on the King/Kittitas county line. Composed of Swauk Formation sandstone, it is best known for its SE "Mega Slab" — one of the longest continuous sandstone slabs in the lower 48 — and its complex, no-easy-way-up terrain. It was first climbed August 8, 1939 by Fred Beckey, Joe Barto, and Wayne Swift via the Southwest Face.

routes.overview: A 3-pitch (up to 5.6) summit block sits atop roughly 1,000 ft of Class 3-4 gully scrambling on the SW Face; this is Bears Breast's original 1939 first-ascent line and its standard route, reached via a long approach to Dutch Miller Gap.
routes.beta: From a camp near Dutch Miller Gap, ascend the left-hand of two parallel gullies on the SW Face (~1,000 ft, Class 3-4) to a ridge notch NW of the true summit, then climb the summit block in three short pitches (up to 5.6) to the top. Descend via three single-rope rappels and reverse the gully.
routes.fa: Fred Beckey, Joe Barto, Wayne Swift — August 8, 1939 (Southwest Face)
routes.rappels: 3 single-rope rappels (one 50m rope sufficient; 60m or two 50m ropes preferable for 3-person parties)
objHaz: Loose, broken rock through the gully and lower pitches
Marginal protection near an old, loose piton on the Pitch 1 crux
Slow, complex route-finding through ledges on descent
routes.season: Jul-Sep (approach and gullies typically hold snow into early summer; documented ascents cluster late Aug-early Sep)
routes.approach: From North Bend, take I-90 to exit 34, follow 468th Ave SE north 0.6 mi, then turn right onto Middle Fork Road (FS Road 56). Continue 12 miles (paved becoming rough) to where the road veers right, then drive ~5 more rough, high-clearance-recommended miles to the Dutch Miller Gap trailhead (old roadbed terminus, ~2,850 ft). Hike the Middle Fork Snoqualmie / Williams Creek corridor roughly 15 miles to Dutch Miller Gap (~4.8 hrs), then continue to a camp below the SW Face. Total round-trip trail distance for the outing is about 22 miles.
routes.descent: Reverse the route: 3 single-rope rappels down the summit block to the notch, then downclimb the Class 3-4 gully system back toward Dutch Miller Gap.
routes.descentText: From the summit, make three single-rope rappels (a single 50m rope is sufficient) down the west side of the summit block to the ridge notch, then downclimb the Class 3-4 gully system back to the base of the face. Route-finding through the ledges is the crux of the descent and is reported to be slower than the ascent (~4.7 hrs summit-to-camp vs. ~6.0 hrs camp-to-summit). Retrace the approach through Dutch Miller Gap and the Middle Fork/Williams Creek trail back to the trailhead.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Drive to the Dutch Miller Gap Trailhead and hike ~15 miles via the Middle Fork Snoqualmie/Williams Creek corridor to Dutch Miller Gap (~4.8 hrs), then continue to a camp below the SW Face. Day 2: Climb the SW Face — ~1,000 ft of gully scrambling then the 3-pitch summit block (~6 hrs camp-to-summit) — and descend via rappels and downclimbing back to camp (~4.7 hrs). Day 3: Hike back out to the trailhead.
routes.bail: Below the summit block, retreat down the Class 3-4 gully is straightforward (reverse the ascent line). Once on the roped summit block, the only reasonable retreat is rappelling — the same three single-rope (50m) rappels used for the normal descent; there is no walk-off partway up the block.
routes.turnaround: This is a long, multi-day objective with a slow, route-finding-heavy descent — trip reports record the down-climb taking nearly as long as the ascent. Turn around with enough daylight margin to complete the rappels and lower gully in full light, and account for the multi-hour hike back to camp afterward.
routes.bestSeason: Climbed successfully in late August/early September in the available trip report, with the original first ascent on August 8, 1939; the approach and gullies typically hold snow into early summer, so July through September is the practical window.
routes.detailedRack: Ten stoppers, two small cams, and six runners were adequate for the whole route in the source trip report. A single 50m rope is enough for the rappels, but 60m or two 50m ropes let 3-person parties reach belay stances more comfortably. Rock shoes recommended for the summit block.
routes.proNeeds: Protection is minimal but adequate — small cams and stoppers protect the crack/dihedral pitch. The crux first pitch has marginal protection near an old, loose piton in broken rock; extra care is warranted there.
gear: 50m rope (60m or double 50m ropes preferable for 3-person parties)

pitchDetail.notes: ~1,000 ft up the left-hand of two parallel gullies NE of Dutch Miller Gap; continuous 3rd-4th class scrambling on ledges and short steps; the gully narrows and steepens about 1/3 of the way up.
Above the constriction, angle eases to a distinctive 'white fan' of chunky rock, then traverse right across heather and broken rock to the ridge crest at the second notch NW of the summit block.
Steep, slightly bulging rock; crux with marginal protection near an old, loose piton and broken rock. Ends at a roomy belay ledge.
20-ft crack/dihedral using stemming and heel-toe jams, into a 20-ft squeeze chimney.
Moderately inclined crack on solid rock with dramatic exposure to the true summit.

road.name: Forest Service Road 56 (Middle Fork Road)
road.status: Paved near I-90, becomes rough unpaved past ~12 miles; high-clearance recommended
road.driveNote: From North Bend: I-90 exit 34, north on 468th Ave SE 0.6 mi, right on FSR 56 (Middle Fork Rd), continue 12 mi to where the road veers right, then ~5 more rough miles to the Dutch Miller Gap trailhead parking.

climate.typical: Marine west coast climate with strong orographic lift; west-facing slopes get heavy, wet winter snow while summers are drier under Pacific high pressure.
climate.spring: Deep, often unstable snowpack; approach gullies typically still snow-filled into June.
climate.summer: Warmest, driest window (Jul-Sep) and the main climbing season; afternoon thunderstorms possible.
climate.fall: Shortening days and increasing storm frequency by October.
climate.winter: Heavy wet snow and avalanche hazard; route not in normal winter climbing condition.

routes.corrections: Mountain Project's area page lists the peak's elevation as 6,809 ft; Peakbagger, Wikipedia/USGS-derived data, and ListsOfJohn all independently agree on 7,197 ft, which is used here. Wikipedia/Peakbagger cite the SW Face's easiest grade as class 5.4, while a detailed 2003 trip report grades the actual crux pitch moves 5.6 — this record uses 5.6 as the more granular, pitch-level source.

routes.overview: A ~3,000-ft low-angle sandstone slab climbs the SE shoulder of Bears Breast from Shovel Creek to ~6,700 ft, followed by ~600 ft of exposed scrambling and one short, loose pitch to the summit block — one of the longest continuous slabs in the Cascades.
routes.beta: A long, remote slab route up the SE shoulder. Reached via a multi-day approach up the Waptus River and Shovel Creek drainages, the ~3,000-ft slab itself is low-angle and mostly climbed unroped, topped by a further ~600 ft of exposed scrambling and one short, loose final pitch to the true summit.
routes.fa: 2005 (party not documented in available online sources)
objHaz: Loose/chossy rock on the final summit-block pitch
Exposed, low-angle friction slab with little to no protection
Long, complex descent through brush and cliff bands to Shovel Lake
routes.season: Aug-Sep (documented ascents cluster in late summer/early fall)
routes.approach: From Cle Elum, drive WA-903 north ~19 miles to the Salmon La Sac Campground entrance; veer right on the gravel road (not into the campground) about 1/4 mile to the Salmon La Sac trailhead (~2,500 ft). Hike the Waptus River Trail #1310 to Waptus Lake, then the PCT southbound to a camp at the bridge over the Waptus River. From there, follow an old, unmaintained path paralleling Shovel Creek to the base of the Mega Slab — about 12 miles one-way and roughly 4.5 hours from the vehicle to the base of the slab.
routes.descent: Downclimb the chimney/scramble on easier terrain back to the top of the slab, then descend to Shovel Lake (~1.5 hrs from the summit block) and retrace the approach via Shovel Creek and the Waptus River/PCT trails.
routes.descentText: From the summit block, downclimb the short final pitch/chimney on easier terrain back onto the slab, then pick a way down through loose dirt, open slabs, brush, and cliff bands to Shovel Lake (about 1.5 hours from the summit block in one trip report). From Shovel Lake, retrace the Shovel Creek path and the Waptus River/PCT trail network back to the Salmon La Sac trailhead.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Drive to Salmon La Sac Trailhead and hike the Waptus River Trail to Waptus Lake, then the PCT south to a camp at the bridge over the Waptus River. Day 2: Follow the unmaintained path along Shovel Creek to the base of the Mega Slab (~4.5 hrs from the vehicle), climb the ~3,000-ft slab to the south shoulder (~80 min) and the ~600-ft summit block scramble to the top, then descend to Shovel Lake (~1.5 hrs) and return to camp. Day 3: Hike back out to the trailhead.
routes.bail: On the slab itself, retreat is by downclimbing the low-angle friction rock the way you came — there is no fixed rappel line reported. On the final summit-block pitch, downclimb the same short pitch/chimney used for the descent rather than committing further if conditions or rock quality look poor.
routes.turnaround: The approach alone is a half-day-plus of hiking and bushwhacking before the slab even begins. Build in enough margin to descend the slab, the loose summit-block pitch, and the brushy Shovel Lake descent while still in daylight, plus the long hike back to the trailhead.
routes.bestSeason: Documented ascents cluster in August and early September; a Labor Day weekend ascent reported dry rock and minimal insects. The long, brushy approach and low-angle slab are best done once high-elevation snow has cleared, roughly July through September.
routes.detailedRack: No formal rack is documented for this route — the slab has been climbed in trail runners with minimal hand use, and the short final summit-block pitch has been soloed (though a rope was carried). A light rack (a few cams/stoppers and slings) plus a rope is a reasonable precaution on the final pitch given its loose rock.
routes.proNeeds: The slab itself is unroped, low-angle friction with essentially no protection placements needed. The final summit-block pitch is short, easy-but-exposed, and chossy — a rope or light gear is prudent even though parties have soloed it.

Rope (recommended for the final summit-block pitch)

climate.typical: This east-of-crest approach is on the drier, more continental side of the Cascades than west-side approaches, with generally lower snowpack.
climate.spring: Higher-elevation slab and summit terrain can still hold snow; lower valley approach is typically accessible.
climate.summer: Warmest, driest window (Jul-Sep); main climbing season for the slab.
climate.fall: Trailhead accessible into early November per the Forest Service, but shortening days and storms increase.
climate.winter: Not in normal climbing condition; snow closes access.
